explanation: hic, haec, hoc = this/these — referring to something near the speaker. Contrast with ille (that, over there) and is/ea/id (he/she/it, more neutral).

hanc is accusative singular feminine of hic/haec/hoc (this). It agrees with puellam (acc. sg. fem.) — the direct object. hic follows a 2nd-declension-like pattern for masculine/neuter but uses irregular forms for accusative.
illum is accusative singular masculine of ille/illa/illud (that). It agrees with librum (acc. sg. masc.). ille often refers to something further away or already mentioned.

sentence: vir quī in hortō sedēbat erat pater meus.
display: vir _____ in hortō sedēbat erat pater meus.
translation: The man who was sitting in the garden was my father.
explanation: quī is nominative singular masculine of the relative pronoun qui/quae/quod (who/which). It is the subject of the relative clause (sedēbat). The relative pronoun agrees with its antecedent (vir: m. sg.).

translation: The slave whom the master had praised fled.
explanation: quem is accusative singular masculine of quī — the relative pronoun is the object of laudāverat within the relative clause. The case of the relative pronoun is determined by its function in the relative clause, not by its antecedent.

translation: The king himself entered the temple.
explanation: ipse/ipsa/ipsum = himself/herself/itself/themselves (emphatic). Here ipse (nominative masc. sg.) emphasises rēx: the king himself. Do not confuse with is/ea/id (he/she/it).
huius = of this (genitive singular of hic/haec/hoc, all genders). Note: all three genders share the same genitive singular form huius. Compare: illius (gen. sg. of ille) and eius (gen. sg. of is).
ipsa is nominative singular feminine of ipse/ipsa/ipsum, used here for emphasis: the queen herself. It agrees with rēgīna (f. nom. sg.).

sentence: quārum is _____ of the relative pronoun.
display: quārum is _____ of the relative pronoun.
explanation: quārum is genitive plural feminine of quī/quae/quod — 'whose' or 'of whom'. Here: the girls whose books I read. The genitive plural of quī is quōrum (m./n.) or quārum (f.).

translation: The son of that man was famous.
explanation: illīus is genitive singular of ille/illa/illud (all genders share this form). 'Of that man' — the genitive shows possession. illīus is a common exam form to recognise.
